Title: Overexpression of GA20-OXIDASE1 impacts plant height, biomass allocation and saccharification efficiency in maize

Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: supplies immediate precursors for bioactive GA accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: promotes plant growth, cell division and cell expansion; indirectly affects lignocellulosic biomass traits relevant to bioethanol feedstock
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Increased endogenous GA levels, increased plant height, shifted biomass allocation toward stems/cobs, increased cellulose and lignin deposition, and altered saccharification efficiency of maize stem biomass.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Constitutive overexpression of AtGA20-OX1 in maize increased plant height in greenhouse and field conditions. 2. In greenhouse-grown plants, stem dry weight and total stover increased, while leaves contributed less biomass. 3. Stem cell wall residue, cellulose, and lignin contents increased, with earlier secondary cell wall deposition during internode development. 4. Alkali-pretreated biomass showed higher glucose release per dry weight, whereas acid or hot-water pretreatment gave slower cellulose-to-glucose conversion, likely due to elevated lignin.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: transgenic overexpression in maize; greenhouse and field phenotyping; cellulose and lignin biochemical assays; phloroglucinol staining; saccharification assays under multiple pretreatments; developmental RT-qPCR of lignin-pathway genes
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: greenhouse versus field conditions; year effects in 2012 and 2013 field trials; hot-water, HCl, and NaOH pretreatments for saccharification
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper explicitly describes GA20-OX1 as the rate-limiting step in GA biosynthesis. Biomass increase was robust in greenhouse but not consistent across two field years, indicating strong genotype × environment interaction. The focal nutrient-related output is lignocellulosic biomass quality for saccharification rather than a classical dietary nutrient.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: stems became more slender; leaf dry weight decreased; higher lignin slowed cellulose-to-glucose conversion after acid or hot-water pretreatment; biomass gain in field was inconsistent across years
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a rate-limiting step in GA biosynthesis by the paper
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Useful for engineering taller plants with increased stem biomass and improved glucose yield after alkaline pretreatment, but deployment should consider environmental instability of biomass yield and elevated lignin-associated recalcitrance; may be best combined with strategies optimizing lignin composition or pretreatment compatibility.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: RT-qPCR expression profiling of selected lignin biosynthetic genes; no public accession reported
